plainMeaning: Lord Nemi, the shining jewel of the Yādava family, became a monk, gained perfect knowledge, and reached final liberation all on this one mountain (Girnar) — so may this thrice-holy holy place ever flourish.
recitation: jāyava kula siri tilao, nemī vaya-ggahaṇa ṇāṇa nivvāṇo; jahiṁ patto so naṁdau, ujjaṁto tiguṇam iha titthaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jāyava < Sanskrit yādava, 'descendant of Yadu'; the royal dynasty into which Nemi was born.
wordByWord.notes: First member of the compound jāyava-kula-siri-tilao.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit siri < Sanskrit śrī, 'lustre, majesty, fortune'.
Prakrit tilao < Sanskrit tilaka, the auspicious forehead-mark; figuratively the 'finest adornment' of a thing.
wordByWord.notes: Nominative singular masculine in apposition to Nemi: 'the very tilaka of the glory of the Yādava clan'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit nemi, 'rim of a wheel'; cousin of Kṛṣṇa, born in the Yādava line.

wordByWord.gloss: vow, the great vows (vrata) of renunciation
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vaya < Sanskrit vrata, 'vow'; here the taking of the mahāvratas at dīkṣā.
wordByWord.notes: In this kalyāṇaka-list vaya is vrata (vow), not vāc (speech); see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gahaṇa (doubled to -ggahaṇa in compound) < Sanskrit grahaṇa, 'seizing, adopting'.
wordByWord.notes: vaya-ggahaṇa = 'taking of the vow' = renunciation (dīkṣā), the first of the three events named.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ṇāṇa < Sanskrit jñāna, 'knowledge'; in context the supreme kevala-jñāna (omniscience).
Prakrit nivvāṇa < Sanskrit nirvāṇa, 'blowing out, extinction of karma'.
wordByWord.notes: Third event: liberation. The three together (vow-taking, knowledge, nirvāṇa) are the three kalyāṇakas located here.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jahiṁ < Sanskrit yatra / yasmin, relative locative.
wordByWord.notes: Relative adverb answered by the place-name Ujjayanta in line 2.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit patto < Sanskrit prāpta, past participle of pra-√āp, 'to reach, obtain'.
wordByWord.notes: Subject is Nemi (so), object the three attainments.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit so < Sanskrit saḥ, nominative singular masculine demonstrative.

wordByWord.gloss: may it flourish / be blessed / rejoice
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit naṁdau < Sanskrit nandatu, imperative/benedictive of √nand, 'to thrive, prosper'.
wordByWord.notes: The benedictive refrain of the hymn; its subject is the tīrtha (Ujjayanta).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ujjaṁta < Sanskrit ujjayanta, ancient name of Mount Girnar near Junagadh, Gujarat.
wordByWord.notes: Spelling ઉજજંતો with single-spaced conjunct retained verbatim from the book.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ti-guṇa < Sanskrit tri-guṇa, 'triple'; here qualifying the tīrtha as thrice-holy.
wordByWord.notes: Sandhi: tiguṇam + iha. 'Threefold' because three kalyāṇakas of Nemi occurred here.
Predicate noun: Ujjayanta is the thrice-holy tīrtha.

literalTranslation: The crest-ornament of the glory of the Yādava clan — Nemi — [whose] vow-taking, knowledge, and nirvāṇa [happened here]: where that one attained [them], may it flourish — Ujjayanta, the thrice-holy tīrtha, here.
idiomaticTranslation: Nemi, the crowning jewel of the Yādava clan's glory — here he took the great vows of renunciation, here he won omniscience, and here he reached nirvāṇa. May that Ujjayanta (Girnar), thrice-hallowed by these three blessed events, ever flourish as a holy tīrtha!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse opens the Girnar (Ujjayanta / Raivatagiri) sequence, turning from Ṛṣabha's Aṣṭāpada in the verses just before to the mountain of the 22nd Jina. Nemi is fixed at once in his lineage: he is the 'tilaka' — the auspicious crest-mark, the very finest adornment — of the śrī (glory) of the jāyava (Yādava) clan, the dynasty of Kṛṣṇa. The first line then strings together three of Nemi's life-events in their natural sequence: vaya-ggahaṇa (the taking of the great vows, i.e. renunciation/dīkṣā), ṇāṇa (the rise of kevala-jñāna, omniscience), and nivvāṇa (final liberation). The second line resolves the relative jahiṁ ('where'): the single place where 'he attained' all three is named — Ujjayanta. The closing word tiguṇa ('threefold') is the verse's hinge: Girnar is hallowed not once but three times over, precisely because three of Nemi's five kalyāṇakas occurred on this one summit. (His conception and birth belong to the Śauripura/Dvārakā region, which is why the mountain is 'thrice-' and not 'fivefold-' holy.) The benedictive naṁdau — the hymn's recurring gesture — is here directed at the tīrtha itself: 'may it flourish.' The poet thus compresses an entire hagiography into a single breath: clan, renunciation, enlightenment, liberation, place, and blessing.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The triad vaya-ggahaṇa → ṇāṇa → nivvāṇa mirrors the Jain ascent to mokṣa: adoption of right conduct (cāritra) through the mahāvratas, then the annihilation of the four ghātī (obscuring) karmas yielding kevala-jñāna, and finally the shedding of the remaining aghātī karmas at nirvāṇa. A site's sanctity in Jain thought derives from such kalyāṇaka events imprinted on it; naming three of them justifies calling Girnar tiguṇa-tittha, a 'thrice-holy crossing'. The verse also quietly contrasts two Yādava destinies — Nemi the liberated Jina versus his cousin Kṛṣṇa the worldly hero — underscoring renunciation as the clan's true glory.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the worshipper bows in mind to Girnar without travelling there, and rehearses the very pattern of liberation — renounce, awaken, be freed — as a template for personal practice. The single mountain holding all three events teaches that one steadfast place of effort can carry a soul the whole way.

crossReferences.text: Uttarādhyayana Sūtra, ch. 22 (Rathanemi / Rājimatī — Nemi's renunciation)
crossReferences.source: Śvetāmbara Āgama; Jacobi tr., Sacred Books of the East vol. 45

sources.title: Neminatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Nemi's Yādava lineage, kinship with Kṛṣṇa, renunciation and liberation at Girnar

sources.title: Girnar — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Ujjayanta / Raivatagiri identification with Girnar and its status as the site of Nemi's dīkṣā, kevalajñāna and nirvāṇa

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the Prakrit tokens: vrata, grahaṇa, jñāna, nirvāṇa, prāpta, tilaka, yādava, nand, tri-guṇa

notes: vaya is read as vrata (vow → renunciation/dīkṣā), not vāc (speech): the surrounding triad with ṇāṇa and nivvāṇa fixes it as the first of three kalyāṇakas. tiguṇa ('threefold') refers to those three kalyāṇakas at Girnar — Nemi's conception and birth kalyāṇakas occurred elsewhere (Śauripura/Dvārakā), hence 'thrice-' and not 'fivefold-' holy. Orthography preserved verbatim (e.g. ઉજજંતો with its single-spaced conjunct; ગ્ગહણ with doubled g- in compound). Sandhi tiguṇam-iha split into tiguṇam + iha for glossing.
